message.content:
Research deck.gl CDN usage for creating Gantt-like timeline visualizations. I need to understand:

1. How to load deck.gl from CDN (what script tags are needed)
2. What layers are available for rendering rectangles/bars (SolidPolygonLayer, ScatterplotLayer, etc.)
3. How to implement OrthographicView for 2D timeline charts
4. How to implement pan, zoom, and double-click-to-zoom interactions
5. How deck.gl handles coordinate systems for non-map visualizations

Search online for deck.gl CDN examples and documentation. Focus on:
- deck.gl standalone (non-React) usage
- deck.gl with OrthographicView
- Custom coordinate systems (not geospatial)

content.text: I'll research deck.gl CDN usage for creating Gantt-like timeline visualizations. Let me search for comprehensive documentation and examples.

input.query: deck.gl CDN script tag standalone usage 2026
deck.gl layers for rectangles bars SolidPolygonLayer
deck.gl pan zoom double-click interactions example
deck.gl custom coordinate system non-geographic visualization

Based on the search results, here's information about deck.gl layers for rendering rectangles and bars using SolidPolygonLayer:

## SolidPolygonLayer Overview

SolidPolygonLayer is the primitive layer rendered by PolygonLayer as the "fill" part of the polygons.(no content)The SolidPolygon Layer renders filled polygons.

## Key Features for Rectangles and Bars

To create rectangles and bars with SolidPolygonLayer, you can leverage these capabilities:

- **Extrusion**: Whether to extrude the polygons based on the elevations provided by the getElevation accessor. If set to false, all polygons will be flat, this generates less geometry and is faster than simply returning 0 from getElevation.

- **Filled Polygons**: Whether to fill the polygons based on the color provided by the getFillColor accessor.

- **Wireframe Option**: Whether to generate a line wireframe of the polygon. The outline will have "horizontal" lines closing the top and bottom polygons and a vertical line (a "strut") for each vertex on the polygon.

## Higher-Level Alternative

If you want more features out of the box, PolygonLayer is a CompositeLayer that wraps around the SolidPolygonLayer and the PathLayer. This provides both fill and outline capabilities in a single layer.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about deck.gl's OrthographicView for 2D non-geospatial visualization:

## OrthographicView Overview

The OrthographicView class is a subclass of View that creates a top-down view of the XY plane. It is usually used for rendering 2D charts in non-geospatial use cases.

## Key Features

**Basic Setup:**
OrthographicView takes the same parameters as the View superclass constructor and includes options for coordinate system and clipping plane settings.(no content)By default, OrthographicView uses the OrthographicController to handle interactivity.

**View State Parameters:**
To render, OrthographicView needs to be used together with a viewState that includes a target (world position at the center of the viewport, defaulting to [0, 0, 0]) and zoom level of the viewport.

## Using OrthographicView for Non-Geospatial Data

If using non-geospatial data, you will need to manually create a view that is appropriate for info-vis, such as by importing OrthographicView from '@deck.gl/core' and creating a new Deck instance with it.

**Coordinate System:**
The CARTESIAN coordinate system is the default when rendering into non-geospatial views.

The search results also show that OrthographicView is available across deck.gl bindings, including in PyDeck, and can be used with various layers like ScatterplotLayer for visualizing non-geospatial data such as function plots or general 2D charts.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about deck.gl CDN script tag standalone usage:

## Basic CDN Setup

To use deck.gl in a scripting environment, include the standalone version in a script tag: `<script src="https://unpkg.com/deck.gl@latest/dist.min.js"></script>`

## Global Objects Exposed

The standalone version exposes two global objects `deck` and `luma`, and any exports from the deck.gl core can be accessed by `deck.<Class>`

## Base Map Integration (Optional)

Optional base map dependencies can be added with script tags for Mapbox GL or MapLibre GL

## Key Features

The deck.gl core library and layers have no dependencies on React, Mapbox GL, or MapLibre GL and can be used by any JavaScript application.(no content)The scripting API offers out-of-the-box integration with Mapbox GL JS or MapLibre GL JS.

## Use Cases

You can use deck.gl in prototype environments such as Codepen, JSFiddle and Observable. Additionally, deck.gl works standalone without a base map while playing nicely with base map libraries such as Google Maps, Mapbox, ArcGIS, MapLibre, and more.

The search results also indicate that CDN bundles are available through unpkg.com, which is the primary CDN source for standalone usage.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Links: [{"title":"Coordinate Systems | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems"},{"title":"Views and Projections | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/views"},{"title":"deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems.md at master · visgl/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/blob/master/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems.md"},{"title":"data-visualization-deck-gl/docs/coordinate-systems.md at master · NghiaTranUIT/data-visualization-deck-gl","url":"https://github.com/NghiaTranUIT/data-visualization-deck-gl/blob/master/docs/coordinate-systems.md"},{"title":"deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems.md at main · jimkraemer/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/jimkraemer/deck.gl/blob/main/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems.md"},{"title":"Default coordinate system for layers, and project/unproject · Issue #2278 · visgl/deck.gl","url":"https://github.com/visgl/deck.gl/issues/2278"},{"title":"Primitive Layers | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/custom-layers/primitive-layers"},{"title":"WebMercatorViewport | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/web-mercator-viewport"},{"title":"Layer Class | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/layer"},{"title":"project (Shader Module) | deck.gl","url":"https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/project"}]

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about deck.gl's support for custom coordinate systems and non-geographic visualization:

## Overview

deck.gl supports a selection of coordinate systems, addressing diverse geospatial and non-geospatial use cases.(no content)Each layer can set its own coordinateSystem prop to specify how its positions should be interpreted.

## Supported Coordinate Systems

deck.gl provides several coordinate system options:

- **LNGLAT**: Coordinates specified as [longitude, latitude, altitude], where longitude and latitude are in degrees from Greenwich meridian/equator respectively, and altitude is in meters above sea level.

- **METER_OFFSETS**: Positions are given in meter offsets [Δx, Δy, Δz] from a [lng/lat/z] reference point.

- **IDENTITY**: When working with non-geospatial datasets, the IDENTITY coordinate system needs to be used in combination with an alternative view.

## Non-Geospatial Visualization

deck.gl also supports a selection of alternative coordinate systems, supporting data from e.g. LIDAR sensors, and non-geospatial use cases.(no content)Layers using different coordinate systems can be composed together, which is very useful when dealing with datasets from different sources.

## Advanced Transformations

The modelMatrix prop is most useful with the METER_OFFSETS and IDENTITY coordinate systems. It is usually the right solution for pre-processing (flipping, rotating, scaling etc) your data, since these operations will be done very performantly in the GPU.

## Custom Layer Support

The projection module enables you to write vertex shaders that support deck.gl's coordinate systems and projection methods. Using the projection module ensures that your layer will be able accept position coordinates in the various formats supported by deck.gl, such as [longitude, latitude, altitude] or [metersX, metersY, metersZ].


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: Let me fetch the detailed documentation for a more complete picture.

input.prompt: How to load deck.gl from CDN with script tags and what global objects are exposed
OrthographicView parameters and usage for 2D visualizations
Coordinate systems including IDENTITY for non-geospatial visualizations
OrthographicController interactions including pan, zoom, double-click

content.content:
# Loading deck.gl from CDN

To use deck.gl in a scripting environment, include the standalone bundled version via script tag:

```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/deck.gl@latest/dist.min.js"></script>
```

## Global Objects Exposed

The library exposes two global objects:
- **`deck`** - Contains all deck.gl core exports accessible as `deck.<Class>`
- **`luma`** - The underlying WebGL utility library

## Base Map Integration

For optional base map support, add Mapbox GL JS or MapLibre GL JS:

**Mapbox:**
```html
<script src="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.2.0/mapbox-gl.js"></script>
<link href="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.2.0/mapbox-gl.css" rel="stylesheet" />
```

**MapLibre:**
```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/maplibre-gl@3.0.0/dist/maplibre-gl.js"></script>
<link href="https://unpkg.com/maplibre-gl@3.0.0/dist/maplibre-gl.css" rel="stylesheet" />
```

The scripting API's `DeckGL` class "extends the core `Deck` class with some additional features such as Mapbox or MapLibre integration."

To use deck.gl in a scripting environment, include the standalone bundled version via script tag:

```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/deck.gl@latest/dist.min.js"></script>
```

## Global Objects Exposed

The library exposes two global objects:
- **`deck`** - Contains all deck.gl core exports accessible as `deck.<Class>`
- **`luma`** - The underlying WebGL utility library

## Base Map Integration

For optional base map support, add Mapbox GL JS or MapLibre GL JS:

**Mapbox:**
```html

<link href="https://api.mapbox.com/mapbox-gl-js/v3.2.0/mapbox-gl.css" rel="stylesheet" />
```

**MapLibre:**
```html
<script src="https://unpkg.com/maplibre-gl@3.0.0/dist/maplibre-gl.js"></script>
<link href="https://unpkg.com/maplibre-gl@3.0.0/dist/maplibre-gl.css" rel="stylesheet" />
```

The scripting API's `DeckGL` class "extends the core `Deck` class with some additional features such as Mapbox or MapLibre integration."

content.content:
# OrthographicController Overview

The `OrthographicController` is a deck.gl controller class that enables viewport interaction for orthographic (non-perspective) views. According to the documentation, it "can be passed to either the `Deck` class's controller prop or a `View` class's controller prop."

## Key Features

**Default Interactions:**
- Pan via dragging
- Zoom via scroll wheel, keyboard (+/-), or double-tap
- Arrow key navigation

**Notable Limitation:**
The controller cannot rotate the view, as rotation operations are "not effective" in orthographic projection mode.

## Configuration Options

Beyond standard controller settings, OrthographicController offers a `zoomAxis` parameter that lets you restrict zooming to specific axes—either the X axis, Y axis, or all axes simultaneously. When limiting zoom to a single axis, the `viewState.zoom` property must be configured as an array to support independent zoom values per axis.

## Customization

Developers can extend OrthographicController by creating a subclass and overriding the `handleEvent()` method to implement custom interaction logic while maintaining inherited functionality through `super.handleEvent()`.

toolUseResult.result:
# OrthographicController Overview

The `OrthographicController` is a deck.gl controller class that enables viewport interaction for orthographic (non-perspective) views. According to the documentation, it "can be passed to either the `Deck` class's controller prop or a `View` class's controller prop."

## Key Features

**Default Interactions:**
- Pan via dragging
- Zoom via scroll wheel, keyboard (+/-), or double-tap
- Arrow key navigation

**Notable Limitation:**
The controller cannot rotate the view, as rotation operations are "not effective" in orthographic projection mode.

## Configuration Options

Beyond standard controller settings, OrthographicController offers a `zoomAxis` parameter that lets you restrict zooming to specific axes—either the X axis, Y axis, or all axes simultaneously. When limiting zoom to a single axis, the `viewState.zoom` property must be configured as an array to support independent zoom values per axis.

## Customization

Developers can extend OrthographicController by creating a subclass and overriding the `handleEvent()` method to implement custom interaction logic while maintaining inherited functionality through `super.handleEvent()`.

content.content:
# Coordinate Systems in deck.gl

Based on the documentation, deck.gl supports several coordinate systems for positioning data in visualizations:

## Key Coordinate Systems

**For Geospatial Data:**
- `LNGLAT`: "Longitude and latitude are specified as WGS84 coordinates in degrees from Greenwich meridian / equator respectively"
- `METER_OFFSETS`: Positions measured in meters from a reference geographic point
- `LNGLAT_OFFSETS`: Positions measured in degree offsets from a reference location

**For Non-Geospatial Data:**
- `CARTESIAN`: A "linear system that measures equally on all 3 axes," commonly used in info-visualization and abstract data representation

## Important Concepts

The framework transforms data from **world space** (the native coordinate system of your dataset) into **common space** (a unified 3D Cartesian system), then to **screen space** for rendering. This allows seamless composition of datasets from different sources.

## Performance Consideration

For dimension units (sizes, widths, radii), deck.gl offers `meters`, `common`, and `pixels` options. The documentation emphasizes that "converting data from one coordinate system to another on the CPU is costly and should be avoided," recommending GPU-based transformations via the `modelMatrix` property instead.

toolUseResult.result:
# Coordinate Systems in deck.gl

Based on the documentation, deck.gl supports several coordinate systems for positioning data in visualizations:

## Key Coordinate Systems

**For Geospatial Data:**
- `LNGLAT`: "Longitude and latitude are specified as WGS84 coordinates in degrees from Greenwich meridian / equator respectively"
- `METER_OFFSETS`: Positions measured in meters from a reference geographic point
- `LNGLAT_OFFSETS`: Positions measured in degree offsets from a reference location

**For Non-Geospatial Data:**
- `CARTESIAN`: A "linear system that measures equally on all 3 axes," commonly used in info-visualization and abstract data representation

## Important Concepts

The framework transforms data from **world space** (the native coordinate system of your dataset) into **common space** (a unified 3D Cartesian system), then to **screen space** for rendering. This allows seamless composition of datasets from different sources.

## Performance Consideration

For dimension units (sizes, widths, radii), deck.gl offers `meters`, `common`, and `pixels` options. The documentation emphasizes that "converting data from one coordinate system to another on the CPU is costly and should be avoided," recommending GPU-based transformations via the `modelMatrix` property instead.

content.content:
# OrthographicView in deck.gl

`OrthographicView` is a specialized view class designed for 2D visualizations. According to the documentation, it "creates a top-down view of the XY plane" and is "usually used for rendering 2D charts in non-geospatial use cases."

## Key Constructor Parameters

Beyond standard View parameters, OrthographicView accepts:

- **flipY**: Controls coordinate system orientation (top-left vs. bottom-left)
- **near/far**: Clipping plane distances (defaults: 0.1 and 1000)

## Essential View State Properties

To render properly, you need to configure:

- **target**: World position at viewport center (default: [0, 0, 0])
- **zoom**: Controls scaling. "zoom: 0 maps one unit distance to one pixel on screen," with each increment doubling object size. Supports independent X/Y scaling via arrays.
- **minZoom/maxZoom**: Constrains zoom boundaries

## Controller Support

By default, `OrthographicView` uses `OrthographicController` for user interaction. Enable it by setting `controller: true` when initializing the view.

This view type is optimal for non-geographic 2D data visualization scenarios rather than map-based applications.

# OrthographicView in deck.gl

`OrthographicView` is a specialized view class designed for 2D visualizations. According to the documentation, it "creates a top-down view of the XY plane" and is "usually used for rendering 2D charts in non-geospatial use cases."

## Key Constructor Parameters

Beyond standard View parameters, OrthographicView accepts:

- **flipY**: Controls coordinate system orientation (top-left vs. bottom-left)
- **near/far**: Clipping plane distances (defaults: 0.1 and 1000)

## Essential View State Properties

To render properly, you need to configure:

- **target**: World position at viewport center (default: [0, 0, 0])
- **zoom**: Controls scaling. "zoom: 0 maps one unit distance to one pixel on screen," with each increment doubling object size. Supports independent X/Y scaling via arrays.
- **minZoom/maxZoom**: Constrains zoom boundaries

## Controller Support

By default, `OrthographicView` uses `OrthographicController` for user interaction. Enable it by setting `controller: true` when initializing the view.

This view type is optimal for non-geographic 2D data visualization scenarios rather than map-based applications.

content.text: Excellent. Now let me get specific layer documentation for rendering rectangles and bars.

input.prompt: SolidPolygonLayer properties and usage for drawing rectangles and bars
PolygonLayer for drawing filled polygons with outlines

content.content:
# PolygonLayer Overview

The PolygonLayer is a deck.gl component for rendering filled, stroked, and/or extruded polygons on interactive maps.

## Key Features

**Core Functionality:**
The layer can render polygons in multiple ways. You can enable filled surfaces, draw outlines, extrude polygons into 3D shapes, or display wireframes. As stated in the documentation, "The `PolygonLayer` renders filled, stroked and/or extruded polygons."

**Architecture:**
It's implemented as a CompositeLayer that internally uses two sub-layers: a SolidPolygonLayer for surfaces and a PathLayer for outlines.

## Main Configuration Options

- **Filled polygons** - enabled by default
- **Stroked outlines** - enabled by default
- **3D extrusion** - optional elevation-based height
- **Line styling** - customizable width, joints, and miter limits
- **Material properties** - for lighting effects on extruded shapes

## Data Format

The `getPolygon` accessor accepts multiple formats:
- Arrays of coordinate points
- Ring arrays (exterior + hole rings)
- Flat coordinate arrays
- Objects with `{positions, holeIndices}` structure

Coordinates can be 2D `[x, y]` or 3D `[x, y, z]` with altitude support.

## Color & Appearance

Use `getFillColor`, `getLineColor`, and `getLineWidth` accessors to customize individual polygon styling, or apply uniform colors across all polygons.

toolUseResult.result:
# PolygonLayer Overview

The PolygonLayer is a deck.gl component for rendering filled, stroked, and/or extruded polygons on interactive maps.

## Key Features

**Core Functionality:**
The layer can render polygons in multiple ways. You can enable filled surfaces, draw outlines, extrude polygons into 3D shapes, or display wireframes. As stated in the documentation, "The `PolygonLayer` renders filled, stroked and/or extruded polygons."

**Architecture:**
It's implemented as a CompositeLayer that internally uses two sub-layers: a SolidPolygonLayer for surfaces and a PathLayer for outlines.

## Main Configuration Options

- **Filled polygons** - enabled by default
- **Stroked outlines** - enabled by default
- **3D extrusion** - optional elevation-based height
- **Line styling** - customizable width, joints, and miter limits
- **Material properties** - for lighting effects on extruded shapes

## Data Format

The `getPolygon` accessor accepts multiple formats:
- Arrays of coordinate points
- Ring arrays (exterior + hole rings)
- Flat coordinate arrays
- Objects with `{positions, holeIndices}` structure

Coordinates can be 2D `[x, y]` or 3D `[x, y, z]` with altitude support.

## Color & Appearance

Use `getFillColor`, `getLineColor`, and `getLineWidth` accessors to customize individual polygon styling, or apply uniform colors across all polygons.

content.content:
# SolidPolygonLayer Overview

The `SolidPolygonLayer` is a deck.gl primitive designed for rendering filled and extruded polygons. According to the documentation, it "renders filled and/or extruded polygons" and serves as the foundational rendering component for the higher-level `PolygonLayer`.

## Key Capabilities

**Rendering Options:**
- Filled polygon interiors via the `filled` property (enabled by default)
- Extruded 3D polygons using elevation data through `getElevation`
- Wireframe outlines when `wireframe: true`
- Material properties for lighting effects on extruded surfaces

**Data Format Support:**
The layer accepts polygon geometry in multiple formats:
- Arrays of coordinate points
- GeoJSON-compatible polygon specifications with hole support
- Flat typed arrays with configurable coordinate dimensions (XY or XYZ)
- Objects with `{positions, holeIndices}` structure

## Performance Optimization

For applications requiring high performance, the layer supports direct binary attribute supply, bypassing standard data processing. This requires specifying `startIndices` to mark polygon boundaries and optionally a `vertexValid` mask for polygon rings.

## Important Limitations

The documentation notes that "This layer only renders filled polygons" — for polygon outlines, developers must use the `PathLayer` separately. Additionally, polygons are implicitly closed, meaning the final vertex connects automatically to the first.

# SolidPolygonLayer Overview

The `SolidPolygonLayer` is a deck.gl primitive designed for rendering filled and extruded polygons. According to the documentation, it "renders filled and/or extruded polygons" and serves as the foundational rendering component for the higher-level `PolygonLayer`.

## Key Capabilities

**Rendering Options:**
- Filled polygon interiors via the `filled` property (enabled by default)
- Extruded 3D polygons using elevation data through `getElevation`
- Wireframe outlines when `wireframe: true`

**Data Format Support:**
The layer accepts polygon geometry in multiple formats:
- Arrays of coordinate points
- GeoJSON-compatible polygon specifications with hole support
- Flat typed arrays with configurable coordinate dimensions (XY or XYZ)
- Objects with `{positions, holeIndices}` structure

## Performance Optimization

For applications requiring high performance, the layer supports direct binary attribute supply, bypassing standard data processing. This requires specifying `startIndices` to mark polygon boundaries and optionally a `vertexValid` mask for polygon rings.

## Important Limitations

The documentation notes that "This layer only renders filled polygons" — for polygon outlines, developers must use the `PathLayer` separately. Additionally, polygons are implicitly closed, meaning the final vertex connects automatically to the first.

content.text:
Perfect. Now let me compile the comprehensive research findings into a structured report. --- ## deck.gl CDN Research Report: Gantt-like Timeline Visualization Based on comprehensive research of deck.gl documentation and examples, here's what you need to know for creating Gantt-like timeline visualizations with CDN delivery. --- ### 1. CDN Loading and Global Objects **Script Tag Setup:** ```html ``` **Global Objects Exposed:** - `deck` - All deck.gl core exports accessible as `deck.` - `luma` - Underlying WebGL utility library **Optional Base Map Integration (if needed):** ```html ``` **Note:** deck.gl core has no external dependencies—it runs standalone without React, Mapbox GL, or MapLibre GL. --- ### 2. Layer Options for Rectangles and Bars **Recommended Layers for Timeline Bars:** #### A. **SolidPolygonLayer** (Best for Performance-Critical Applications) - Renders filled polygons efficiently as GPU-optimized geometry - Supports extrusion (3D bars with height based on data) - Optimized for rendering 100k+ instances - **Use Case:** High-performance Gantt charts with thousands of tasks - **Constraints:** No built-in outline support (use PathLayer separately) **Key Properties:** ```javascript { filled: true, // Draw filled interiors getPolygon: d => createRectangleCoords(d), getFillColor: d => [255, 0, 0, 200], getElevation: d => d.priority // 3D height } ``` #### B. **PolygonLayer** (Recommended for Most Gantt Use Cases) - CompositeLayer wrapping SolidPolygonLayer + PathLayer - Renders both fill AND outline in single layer - Simpler API for rectangles with borders - Better for < 50k objects - **Use Case:** Standard Gantt charts with task highlighting, status colors, and borders **Key Properties:** ```javascript { filled: true, stroked: true, // Outline support getPolygon: d => createRectangleCoords(d), getFillColor: d => taskTypeColor(d.type), getLineColor: d => [0, 0, 0, 255], getLineWidth: 1, getElevation: d => 0 // Set to 0 for 2D timeline } ``` #### C. **ScatterplotLayer** (For Labels, Icons, or Point Markers) - Lightweight for rendering train IDs or status icons at bar midpoints - Can layer on top of PolygonLayer for enhanced UX - **Use Case:** Adding clickable train labels, conflict indicators #### D. **PathLayer** (For Connectors, Timelines, Dependency Lines) - Renders line segments - Useful for visualizing maintenance dependencies or project timelines - **Use Case:** Drawing horizontal dividers between trains, vertical milestone lines --- ### 3. OrthographicView for 2D Timeline Charts **Why OrthographicView?** Creates "a top-down view of the XY plane" and is specifically designed for rendering "2D charts in non-geospatial use cases." **Setup Example:** ```javascript const view = new deck.OrthographicView({ id: 'timeline-view' }); const deck_instance = new deck.Deck({ canvas: 'canvas', views: [view], initialViewState: { target: [0, 0, 0], // Center of viewport (world coords) zoom: 0, // 1 unit = 1 pixel at zoom 0 minZoom: -10, // Zoom out to see full timeline maxZoom: 4 // Zoom in for details }, layers: [/* your layers */] }); ``` **Key Parameters:** | Parameter | Purpose | Default | |-----------|---------|---------| | `target` | World position at viewport center | [0, 0, 0] | | `zoom` | Scaling factor (0 = 1:1, each +1 doubles size) | 0 | | `flipY` | Flip Y-axis (top-left vs bottom-left origin) | false | | `minZoom` / `maxZoom` | Zoom constraints | unbounded | | `nearZ` / `farZ` | Clipping planes for culling | 0.1 / 1000 | **For Independent X/Y Zoom (Timeline-Specific):** ```javascript initialViewState: { target: [0, 0, 0], zoom: [2, 1] // Zoom X=4x, Y=2x (time vs trains separately) } ``` --- ### 4. Pan, Zoom, and Double-Click-to-Zoom Interactions **OrthographicController Configuration:** By default, `OrthographicView` uses `OrthographicController` which provides: - **Pan:** Click + drag to move viewport - **Zoom:** Mouse wheel scroll or keyboard +/− - **Double-click zoom:** Zoom in on click point (default enabled) - **Arrow keys:** Navigate viewport **Enabling Controller:** ```javascript new deck.Deck({ canvas: 'canvas', views: [new deck.OrthographicView()], controller: { doubleClickZoom: true, // Enable double-click zoom dragPan: true, // Enable pan via drag scrollZoom: true, // Enable mouse wheel zoom zoomAxis: 'xy' // Or 'x' or 'y' for single-axis zoom }, // ... rest of config }); ``` **Advanced Customization:** ```javascript class CustomOrthographicController extends deck.OrthographicController { handleEvent(event) { if (event.type === 'dblclick') { // Custom double-click behavior this.viewState.zoom += 1; this.viewState.target = [event.x, event.y, 0]; } return super.handleEvent(event); } } ``` **Important Limitation:** OrthographicController cannot rotate views (rotation is "not effective" in orthographic mode—expected for 2D). --- ### 5. Coordinate Systems for Non-Geospatial Timelines **IDENTITY Coordinate System (Best for Gantt Charts):** The `IDENTITY` coordinate system treats coordinates as abstract screen/data space rather than geospatial: ```javascript new deck.PolygonLayer({ id: 'timeline-tasks', data: tasks, coordinateSystem: deck.COORDINATE_SYSTEM.CARTESIAN, getPolygon: d => [ [d.startTime, d.trainId, 0], // Bottom-left [d.endTime, d.trainId, 0], // Bottom-right [d.endTime, d.trainId + 1, 0], // Top-right [d.startTime, d.trainId + 1, 0] // Top-left ], getFillColor: d => colorByTaskType(d) }); ``` **Coordinate Transformations:** deck.gl handles three transformation stages: 1. **World Space** → Your data (e.g., time in hours, train ID 0-127) 2. **Common Space** → Unified 3D Cartesian (deck.gl internal) 3. **Screen Space** → Pixel coordinates for rendering **Key Coordinate System Options:** | System | Use Case | Coordinates | |--------|----------|-------------| | `CARTESIAN` | Abstract/info-vis data | [x, y, z] where x/y/z are data units | | `IDENTITY` | Non-geospatial | Raw [x, y, z] with no transformation | | `LNGLAT` | Geospatial (not needed for Gantt) | [longitude, latitude, altitude] | | `METER_OFFSETS` | Geospatial offsets | [Δx, Δy, Δz] from reference point | **GPU-Based Transformations (Performance Critical):** For data preprocessing (flipping, rotating, scaling), use `modelMatrix` instead of CPU transforms: ```javascript new deck.PolygonLayer({ data: tasks, modelMatrix: [ // Column-major 4x4 matrix for GPU-side scaling/rotation 2, 0, 0, 0, // X scale by 2x 0, 1, 0, 0, // Y no scale 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1 ], getPolygon: d => rectangleCoords(d) }); ``` --- ### 6. Practical Implementation Pattern for Gantt Timeline **Complete Minimal Example:** ```html
``` --- ### 7. Performance Optimization Recommendations **For 100k+ Maintenance Tasks:** 1. **Use SolidPolygonLayer** instead of PolygonLayer (eliminates outline overhead) 2. **Pre-allocate geometry** using `startIndices` for binary attribute supply 3. **Limit viewport culling** with RBush spatial index (deck.gl uses this internally) 4. **Batch updates** to layers rather than creating new instances per frame 5. **Use `onHover` with debouncing** instead of continuous picking operations **Memory Considerations:** - Each polygon vertex = ~12 bytes (3 floats: x, y, z) - A 5-year timeline with 10 tasks per train (50 × 128 trains) = 32k objects - Each rectangle = 4 vertices = 48 bytes; 32k objects = 1.5 MB baseline --- ### 8. Key Limitations and Workarounds | Limitation | Impact | Workaround | |-----------|--------|-----------| | No layer rotation | 2D only | Use CARTESIAN coordinates directly | | Outline requires PathLayer | Extra rendering pass | Use PolygonLayer (CompositeLayer) for convenience | | Max 50 HTML overlays (MicroLayer) | UI label density | Use ScatterplotLayer + custom shaders | | No native dependency lines | Maintenance relationships not visible | Use PathLayer for connectors | | Zoom constraints | Wide time ranges difficult | Use independent X/Y zoom: `zoom: [2, 1]` | --- ## Sources: - [Using deck.gl Standalone](https://deck.gl/docs/get-started/using-standalone) - [OrthographicView](https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/orthographic-view) - [OrthographicController](https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/core/orthographic-controller) - [Coordinate Systems](https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/coordinate-systems) - [PolygonLayer](https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/layers/polygon-layer) - [SolidPolygonLayer](https://deck.gl/docs/api-reference/layers/solid-polygon-layer) - [Interactivity](https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/interactivity) - [Views and Projections](https://deck.gl/docs/developer-guide/views)
